properties.title: 21 George Street, Exmouth (tenanted investment)
properties.body: A semi-detached house on George Street, about 50 metres from Exmouth's high street and a 10-minute walk to the seafront, completely renovated three years ago. Open-plan living/dining area, a newly fitted galley kitchen with dishwasher, utility room, downstairs toilet, laminate flooring throughout the ground floor. Upstairs has two double bedrooms and one single, plus a bathroom with both bath and electric shower. Double glazing, private rear courtyard. Freehold. Currently occupied by tenants — marketed as a turnkey investment opportunity.
properties.why: Submitted directly by Jesse. Well inside budget, freehold, right in Exmouth town centre near the high street and a short walk to the seafront. The catch: it's currently tenanted and marketed as an investment property, so buying it for Jesse to actually live in would likely mean waiting out the tenancy or buying with vacant possession terms agreed separately — worth checking with the agent before getting attached. Parking isn't stated either way.
properties.aiTake: Solidly in-budget, freehold, and right in Exmouth town centre near the high street and a short walk to the seafront — but it's currently tenanted and marketed as an investment flip rather than a home ready to move into, and neither a garden nor parking is confirmed.

parking.note: Listed as "ask agent" — not confirmed either way. Exmouth introduced a residents' parking permit scheme in August 2023 covering parts of the town (including the Colonies area and parts of St Andrews Road) — George Street wasn't specifically named in what turned up, so it's worth checking directly with Devon County Council or the agent whether this address falls inside a permit zone before assuming casual on-street parking is available.

EPC E, Council Tax Band B. Chain-free.

Grade II listed, chain-free, and in Jesse's favourite Exmouth pocket. Zoopla's Cloudflare block meant only one photo could be pulled this time rather than the full gallery — worth a second attempt next sweep.

properties.title: New Street, Exmouth (town-centre terrace)
properties.body: A characterful and beautifully presented 2 double bedroom terrace house in Exmouth town centre, a short walk from the train station and a level walk to the seafront. Entrance hallway, downstairs cloakroom, through sitting/dining room with a fireplace and French doors, and a modern kitchen with integrated appliances. Upstairs both double bedrooms have en-suite facilities. Enclosed rear courtyard garden with gated access, plus a brick utility store and timber shed. Gas central heating, double glazing. Freehold. Council Tax Band B.
properties.why: Jesse drove past this one and liked it. Genuinely characterful for a town-centre terrace (fireplace, French doors, both bedrooms en-suite), freehold, and close to the train station and seafront. The honest trade-off: no garage or dedicated parking at all, which would normally be a hard-requirement miss — flagged plainly rather than hidden.
properties.aiTake: A genuinely characterful freehold terrace right in Exmouth town centre, close to the train station and a level walk to the seafront — but it has no parking at all, which is a real miss against the hard requirement, and both bedrooms being upstairs with no ground-floor room rules out long-term stair-free living.

parking.note: No garage or dedicated parking with the property — confirmed directly in the listing. New Street is in Exmouth town centre; on-street parking here is metered/restricted rather than free, and the nearest pay-and-display options are the town-centre car parks a short walk away (e.g. around the Strand/seafront). Worth checking East Devon's residents' permit scheme for this specific postcode before assuming casual on-street parking is realistic.

Open-plan living room and kitchen with a built-in oven and hob, a double bedroom with wardrobe space, and a modern en-suite shower room. Electric heating and UPVC double glazing throughout. It is a short walk to both Exmouth's seafront and the train station, and it's in Exmouth itself rather than out toward one of the villages. The trade-off is honest: this is a plain modern bungalow, not a characterful find, and the outside space is a courtyard rather than a real garden.

research.question: Does Sidmouth have a public pool or MTB trails nearby? What is the story on this place — has it been on the market before? Why does it seem underpriced, and what might be wrong with it?
research.findings:
Pool/trails: yes to both. Sidmouth Swimming Pool (Ham Lane, LED Community Leisure, 25m/5-lane, public and lane swimming) is about a mile from the property, plus Sidmouth Leisure Centre (Primley Road) for gym/classes. For MTB, Mutters Moor and the Peak Hill/Mutter’s Moor loop are right on Sidmouth’s doorstep (linking to Woodbury Castle further west), and there are 80+ mapped trails in the wider East Devon AONB around Salcombe Regis/Hawkerland/Fire Beacon Hill — genuinely better MTB access than most of the list, just not Woodbury Common itself.

The story: this is a real, well-documented building, not an obscure flip. It’s Grade II listed, built c.1817 for the Honiton & Sidmouth Turnpike Trust, and has belonged to East Devon (originally Sidmouth Urban District) Council since 1936. It was used as council social housing from the end of WWII, then stood empty for several years after a severe flood in July 1968 left it unfit for habitation, before the council refurbished it in 1974. It’s now being sold as one of 168 lots in Clive Emson’s July 2026 auction, by instruction of East Devon District Council — this reads as a routine council asset disposal (councils regularly auction off small, hard-to-modernise former-tenancy stock), not a distressed or scandal sale. I couldn’t find any record of this specific building being on the market a year ago — you may be thinking of Ye Olde Toll House in Newton Poppleford, a different (unrelated) toll house that Clive Emson auctioned separately in an earlier sale.

Why it looks underpriced: three real, boring reasons rather than one hidden catch. (1) It’s genuinely tiny — one bed, single-storey — which caps value regardless of location. (2) It’s Grade II listed, so any alterations need listed building consent, which narrows the buyer pool and limits what a buyer could do with it. (3) Auction guide prices are deliberately set low to spark bidding — £95,000 is a floor to draw interest, not a prediction of the final hammer price, which very plausibly lands well above it once bidding opens. On top of that, standard Common Auction Conditions mean it sells as-seen with no room to negotiate on condition, which filters out mortgage-dependent buyers and caps the price a normal owner-occupier pool would push it to — auction stock this small and this specific tends to go to cash buyers.

One genuine thing worth checking before bidding: the building sits right at the entrance to the Byes riverside walk along the River Sid, and its own history includes a severe flood in 1968 that made it uninhabitable for years. The River Sid at Sidmouth still has an active flood warning area today (there was real flooding in the area as recently as January/February 2026), and although Salcombe Road itself isn’t named in the current warning zone (that covers Primley Gardens, Mill Street, Riverside Road, York Street, Ham Lane, East Street and Fore Street), a riverside property with this specific flood history is worth a direct check on the gov.uk flood risk checker by postcode before bidding — not a dealbreaker, just due diligence given the building’s own past.

History of the Old Toll House (Sidmouth Herald)
River Sid at Sidmouth flood warning area (gov.uk)

properties.title: Farleys, The Strand, Lympstone (Grade II cottage)

properties.why: Location-wise this is arguably the best-placed find yet: Lympstone sits on the Exe estuary directly between Exmouth and Woodbury Common — water one way, trails the other, and one of your brothers is in Woodbury just up the hill. Genuine period character (Grade II, beams, stonework), chain-free, and £5k under ceiling. Your call. Also nudged down slightly for price — £295k is into the range you'd call over budget even though it's under the £300k hard ceiling.

research.question: Look further into what you actually do for parking on The Strand, Lympstone.
research.findings: The Strand itself has no dedicated resident bays — parking there is on-street with no confirmed residents' zone. Two car parks serve the immediate area: the free Lympstone Village Station car park right on The Strand (13 spaces, no permit needed, first-come-first-served, but can fill up with rail commuters), and Underhill Car Park a short walk away. Underhill's management has been unsettled for a while — East Devon District Council was in talks with Lympstone Parish Council years ago about the Parish taking it over and potentially issuing permits, and I couldn't confirm what, if anything, was finalised or whether a strict residents' scheme runs there now. Beyond that, East Devon District Council sells modular monthly/annual town permits for its pay-and-display car parks generally, but I couldn't confirm Lympstone is one of the towns those permits cover, nor find a price. Devon County Council handles on-street residents' permit zones separately and I couldn't confirm one exists for The Strand either. Bottom line: there's workable free/cheap parking nearby (the station car park is the best bet), but nothing deeded to the property and nothing guaranteed — worth a direct call to Lympstone Parish Council or East Devon's parking team before relying on it.

properties.title: Circular Thatched Cliff-top Cottage, Peak Hill Road, Sidmouth
properties.body: A unique circular thatched house on a cliff-top position along the Jurassic Coast, designed in the 1960s for novelist R.F. Delderfield. Panoramic sea views over Ladram Bay, mature terraced gardens. Auction closes 21 July 2026 — 8 days from this update.
properties.why: Was Jesse's stated dream home, dropped 2026-07-06 once the erosion risk was actually looked into. Local press confirms the garden has already lost ground to a cliff fall, not just theoretical risk, and that stretch of Peak Hill has a well-documented run of landslips. Separately, standard UK buildings insurance excludes coastal erosion outright, there's no government backstop for it like there is for flooding, and lenders increasingly treat erosion-risk properties as unmortgageable — the only public money available is a £6,000 demolition grant if it's ever condemned, nothing toward lost value. That combination is why the guide price is so low, and why Jesse walked away. Kept here for the record rather than deleted.

research.question: What do the papers/local chatter say about the cliff collapse and why this house is going so cheap, before dismissing it entirely?
research.findings: The cottage lost part of its garden to a confirmed cliff fall in 2024 and now sits roughly 40ft (12m) from the edge of a 120m drop. The coastline here (Sidmouth Mudstone Formation, capped with Cretaceous Greensand at Peak Hill) is soft and fails unpredictably rather than eroding gradually — there's been a steady run of rockfalls along this stretch, including a large one in October 2024 and another as recently as late June 2026, serious enough that East Devon council has closed several nearby cliff-top paths as a precaution. A full cliff survey exists in the auction legal pack. All the press coverage traces back to the same wire story (Sidmouth Herald/Midweek Herald/Exmouth Journal all ran it) — none of it quotes an engineer, the council, or a neighbour on the specific risk, and no Facebook/community-forum chatter about this particular house turned up; it doesn't appear to be a locally-discussed listing. On whether stabilising the cliff (Jesse's caisson idea) is even feasible: no Peak-Hill-specific policy was found, but this is open, undeveloped cliff west of the harbour — the kind of coastline Shoreline Management Plans typically mark 'no active intervention', meaning little chance of public money or planning support for private hard defences. That's an inference from how these plans usually treat isolated clifftop properties, not a confirmed answer for this plot — worth a direct call to East Devon's coastal protection team before spending anything on engineering feasibility.

properties.aiTake: Character and privacy are off the charts, but declined for good reason — the erosion/insurance/mortgage problem is real, and it's the least practical for trains, pools, or trails of anything on the list.

parking.note: Not specifically checked — a cliff-top position suggests limited space, but this wasn't confirmed either way. Moot given the property was declined for erosion/insurance reasons.

properties.aiTake: The cheapest genuinely characterful property found all search — Grade II, freehold, chain-free, and close to a brother and the Common's trails — but it's compact (one double bedroom plus a study) with on-street parking only and a first-floor bedroom that rules out easy ground-floor adaptation.

research.question: Please do a deep dive: the roof looks a little bad on the back. Would love to find out about parking on this street — seems strangely empty in the photos but no off-street parking shown, what do they do? Also what's behind it — if I have my bike in the shed can I take it out of the back of the house behind that deck somewhere, or do I have to traipse the bike through the house? Check repair issues, permissions, and last sale prices/history.
research.findings: Roof: the listing text says nothing about roof condition either way — can't confirm or rule out what was spotted in the photos from the description alone. Genuinely needs eyes-on or a direct question to the agent, especially given Grade II status limits what repairs are straightforward/permitted. Parking: confirmed on-street only, nothing off-street mentioned anywhere in the listing. What's behind it / bike access: the listing only describes internal access — "the recently installed kitchen enjoys direct access to the garden" — with wooden steps up to a raised deck. No side gate, path, or external route is mentioned anywhere, and no shed/outbuilding is mentioned in the listing at all. Read at face value, a bike stored back there would go out through the kitchen, not around the side — but agents routinely leave out side-access details that exist in reality, so treat this as unconfirmed rather than definitively no side gate, until a viewing. Sale history: Land Registry has entries for other numbered addresses in the same cluster (2 Church Stile Cottages sold £270k in 2007; 3 Church Stile Cottages shows a logged 2026 sale of £657k, which looks like a likely data error given everything else on the lane — treat with caution) but nothing tying definitively to this exact cottage since the listing doesn't give a house number/name. Repairs/permissions: no planning applications, enforcement notices, or repair-related records found for this address — the only nearby planning activity on the lane is routine tree-pruning consent for 3 Church Stile Cottages, unrelated to structure or roof. Bottom line: nothing here rules it out, but the roof and rear-access questions genuinely can't be answered from the listing alone — worth putting directly to the agent before going further.

Upstairs is a single double bedroom with a walk-in wardrobe. Enclosed rear garden with patio and decking. Freehold. Marketed by East of Exe, Topsham.
properties.why: Cheap, genuinely characterful, freehold, and right in Woodbury near Bro 2 and Woodbury Common's trails — the trade-off is that it's a small one-bed with no off-street parking, so it suits a single-person or downsizer scenario more than a family home.
properties.aiTake: A cheap, genuinely characterful Grade II cottage right in Woodbury near Bro 2 — but it's a tiny one-bed with no off-street parking, so it trades space and convenience for charm and price.

parking.note: On-street parking only, but Woodbury village has a small free short-stay car park (Flower Street) within a couple of minutes' walk, same as noted for the Mirey Lane cottage already tracked — a realistic fallback if the street itself is full.

properties.title: Plot B, Land East of Exmouth Road, Ebford (self-build plot — AUCTION)
properties.price: £75,000 (guide price, plus £1,200 admin fee + £1,200 buyer's premium)

flags.text: SELF-BUILD PLOT — NO PLANNING PERMISSION SECURED
AUCTION 3 AUG 2026 (19 DAYS, National Online Auction)
HAS PARKING (vehicular access established; buyer builds own driveway)

properties.body: An approximately 1-acre (0.40 hectare) plot of land in a semi-rural setting between the villages of Ebford and Exton, just east of Exeter and roughly 15-20 minutes' drive from Exmouth. Established vehicular access from the road. Being sold freehold by national online auction on 3 August 2026, with contracts exchanging immediately on a successful bid. No planning permission is currently in place — the listing describes only 'development potential, subject to the necessary planning permissions and statutory consents', and the sale is subject to a planning overage provision (the legal pack should be reviewed carefully before bidding). A companion Plot A on the same road is also listed separately at the same guide price.
properties.why: A genuine self-build plot, which Jesse has said he likes as a category — cheap, freehold, and buildable-in-principle. But it is genuinely speculative: no planning permission is secured, so there is real risk of paying £75k+fees for land that can't be built on, and Ebford itself is inland/estuary-side rather than coastal, and not especially close to either brother. Flagging it for awareness given the auction date (21 days out) rather than as a strong recommendation.

checklist.uniqueFeature: Undeveloped self-build plot — buyer designs and builds their own home from scratch, subject to planning permission being secured.

properties.aiTake: Appeals purely on the self-build concept Jesse likes — cheap and freehold — but real planning risk plus a location that's not coastal, not near the MTB trails, and not especially close to either brother keep this as a speculative long-shot rather than a strong pick.

parking.note: Not parking in the conventional sense — this is undeveloped land with established vehicular access from the road, so any driveway/parking would be built as part of a future development.

properties.title: Cliff Road, Budleigh Salterton (ground-floor flat, sea views)
properties.body: A genuinely ground-floor, two-double-bedroom apartment in a small modern block (12 units) on Cliff Road, Budleigh Salterton, with a south-facing terrace looking over the communal garden towards the sea. Open-plan sitting room with sea views and patio access, fitted kitchen with appliances, separate WC plus a modern bathroom. One allocated parking space, with additional visitor parking. Direct access to the coastal footpath via the communal gardens. Share of freehold, 999-year lease from 1982 (roughly 955 years remaining), service charge £200/month. Chain-free. EPC C, very low flood risk.
properties.why: Clears the flats bar cleanly — genuinely ground-floor, cheap, chain-free, and with real allocated parking, which is unusual for a sub-£250k flat here. The sea view and direct coastal footpath access from a shared garden are a real plus for a flat at this price. It's a modern block rather than anything characterful, and the communal garden isn't private, but the near-999-year lease means it behaves like freehold in practice. A solid, low-drama fallback rather than a dream find.

checklist.uniqueFeature: South-facing terrace with a sea view plus direct access to the coastal footpath via the communal gardens

properties.aiTake: A genuinely ground-floor, sea-view flat with real allocated parking and a near-freehold-length lease — practical and cheap, if short on character.

parking.note: One allocated parking space included, plus additional visitor parking on site — confirmed in the listing.
